questionText: Prove, by contradiction, that if $x^2 = 2$ then $x$ is irrational. Set out the logical structure of your argument carefully: state clearly the assumption you are contradicting, and use correct notation throughout (in particular the set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers).

steps.description: State the claim and choose a proof strategy.
steps.explanation: The statement is an implication, so a natural strategy is to keep the hypothesis but deny the conclusion and search for a contradiction. If denying the conclusion turns out to be impossible, the implication must be true.

steps.description: Recall what rational and irrational mean.
steps.explanation: A number is rational exactly when it can be written as a ratio of two integers. 'Irrational' simply means 'not rational', so denying the conclusion amounts to asserting that $x$ is rational.

In a proof by contradiction we suppose the opposite of the desired conclusion alongside the given condition. The goal is now to derive something logically impossible from these two suppositions together.

steps.description: Write x as a fraction in lowest terms.
steps.explanation: Any rational number can be reduced so that numerator and denominator share no common factor. Insisting on lowest terms builds in a property that we can later contradict.
Substituting the fraction into $x^2 = 2$ and clearing the denominator turns the problem into a statement about integers, where parity arguments become available.

steps.description: Deduce that p squared is even.
steps.explanation: The right-hand side is $2$ times an integer, so $p^2$ is a multiple of $2$. This gives our first foothold: information about the parity of $p$.

steps.description: Deduce that p itself is even via the contrapositive.
steps.workingLatex: p \text{ odd} \Rightarrow p^2 \text{ odd}; \ \text{so } p^2 \text{ even} \Rightarrow p \text{ even}
steps.explanation: If $p$ were odd, then $p = 2k+1$ gives $p^2 = 2(2k^2+2k)+1$, which is odd. Since $p^2$ is even, $p$ cannot be odd, so $p$ must be even.

steps.description: Write p as twice an integer.
steps.explanation: Being even means $p$ is exactly twice some integer. Naming that integer lets us feed the fact back into the equation and continue the argument.

steps.description: Substitute p = 2m back into the equation.
steps.explanation: Replacing $p$ shows that $q^2$ is also twice an integer. The same structure has reappeared, and that repetition is exactly what will force the contradiction.
By the identical parity argument used for $p$, an even square forces an even root. So $q$ is even as well.

steps.description: Note that p and q share the factor 2.
steps.explanation: Both integers are divisible by $2$, so $2$ is a common factor of them. Their greatest common divisor is therefore at least $2$.
We deliberately chose the fraction in lowest terms, which means their greatest common divisor is $1$. Having it be at least $2$ is a direct contradiction.
A contradiction means one of our suppositions was untenable. The equation $x^2 = 2$ stands, so the faulty supposition must be that $x$ is rational; hence $x$ is irrational.

steps.description: State the conclusion in implication form.
steps.explanation: We assumed the hypothesis and reached the conclusion with no logical escape, which is precisely what the implication asserts. In particular this shows the specific number $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Assuming $x = \tfrac{p}{q}$ in lowest terms forces $p$ and $q$ to both be even, contradicting $\gcd(p,q)=1$. No such rational exists, so $x^2 = 2 \Rightarrow x \notin \mathbb{Q}$; in particular $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational.
